title: Command Pattern — NxGN Actions
purpose: **AI Summary**: Commands are write operations that modify the database. Production command constructors use IDbContextFactory<ActionsDbContext> and every DbContext-backed command execution method accepts optional ActionsDbContext? dbContext after CancellationToken so commands can run independently or participate in a caller-owned transaction. Save commands use the upsert pattern, delete and lifecycle commands return CommandResult variants, expected domain failures return structured CommandError, and transactional audit rows receive the shared context.

examples.name: Command Types Example 1
examples.code:
┌───────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐  ┌───────────────────┐
│  Save{Entity}     │  │  Delete{Entity}   │  │  {Action}{Entity} │
│  Command          │  │  Command          │  │  Command          │
├───────────────────┤  ├───────────────────┤  ├───────────────────┤
│ Upsert (Empty?)   │  │ Bulk by Guid[]    │  │ State transition  │
│ EntityMapper (DI) │  │ ExecuteDeleteAsync│  │ Cascade effects   │
│ CommandResult<    │  │ Explicit tx       │  │ Side effects      │
│ EntityMapperResult│  │ Returns           │  │ Returns           │
│ <T>>              │  │  CommandResult    │  │  CommandResult<T> │
└───────────────────┘  └───────────────────┘  └───────────────────┘

- Post-save side effects (Redis invalidation, audit logging)
examples.code:
1. var mapResult = entityMapper.MapAsync(dtoList, userId, requestLanguageId, defaultLanguageId, ct, db)
2. if empty result (no NewItems, no UpdatedItems) → return Fail(404)
3. if (isNew) → db.AddRange(mapResult.NewItems)
4. Business logic (uniqueness check → Fail(409), guards → Fail(422), cross-entity ops)
5. try { SaveChangesAsync } catch { TOCTOU → Fail(409) }
6. Post-save side effects (audit, cache invalidation)
7. return Ok(mapResult)

examples.code:
Features/Shared/
└── Results.cs                             # CommandResult, CommandResult<T>, CommandError

Features/{Entity}/Shared/Commands/
├── Save{Entity}Command.cs              # Upsert → CommandResult<EntityMapperResult<T>>
├── Delete{Entity}Command.cs            # Bulk delete → CommandResult
├── {Lifecycle}{Entity}Command.cs       # State transition → CommandResult or CommandResult<T>
└── {Action}{Entity}Command.cs          # Domain operation → CommandResult<T>

Features/{Entity}/Shared/Mappers/
└── {Entity}EntityMapper.cs             # DI-registered mapper returning EntityMapperResult<T>

examples.name: Direct DbContext constructor breaks transaction composition
description: Production commands must use IDbContextFactory and an optional shared context parameter; direct scoped DbContext injection prevents callers from composing commands in one transaction and bypasses the retry-safe owned-context pattern.

rules.rule_text: Use `IDbContextFactory<ActionsDbContext>` in command constructors; never inject scoped `ActionsDbContext` directly in production services.
Use the save (upsert) pattern — determine create vs update by `dto.Id == Guid.Empty`
Use DI-registered `EntityMapper` classes that return `EntityMapperResult<T>` for the mapping step in save commands
Save commands return `CommandResult<EntityMapperResult<T>>` — check `Succeeded` before accessing `Value`
Delete commands return `CommandResult` — protection checks return `CommandResult.Fail(...)`
Lifecycle/action commands return `CommandResult` or `CommandResult<T>` — expected errors return `Fail(...)`
Check for empty mapper result on update (entity not found) — return `Fail(404)`
Use `SaveRequest : EntityDTO` inheritance — SaveRequest extends the DTO
Call `AddRange`/`UpdateRange` on `EntityMapperResult` lists
Wrap `SaveChangesAsync` in try/catch for TOCTOU — return `Fail(409)` on unique constraint violation
Use explicit transactions in delete commands
Use `ExecuteDeleteAsync` for hard deletes — never load-and-delete with `RemoveRange`
Use soft delete (set `ISoftDeletable` fields + `SaveChangesAsync`) for ActionEntity, ActionEvidence, ActionConsultation — never physically delete these entities (ADR-0020)
Wrap post-save side effects in individual try/catch blocks (best-effort)
Use a public IDbContextFactory<ActionsDbContext> constructor for production command DI; internal ActionsDbContext constructors are allowed only for focused tests.
Use `Guid.CreateVersion7()` for new entity IDs
Accept ActionsDbContext? dbContext = null after CancellationToken on every DbContext-backed command execution method so callers can compose commands in a shared transaction.
Validate referential integrity before save (entity exists, FK targets valid)
In owned mode, create the working DbContext inside the EF execution-strategy delegate before beginning an explicit transaction.
Check system protection (core/system entities) before mutation
In shared mode, use the supplied DbContext and participate in its current transaction when one exists; do not dispose caller-owned contexts.
Create separate Create/Update commands — use a single Save command
Stage audit rows through `IAuditService.LogAsync(..., dbContext)` when the audit entry must commit or roll back with the aggregate mutation.
Use static extension method mappers — use DI-registered `EntityMapper` classes
Query data for display — that's the [Query pattern](queries.md)
Publish events directly — endpoints handle side effects
Write `AuditLog` entities directly; use `IAuditService` and pass the shared DbContext when the audit entry is transactional.
Use `SaveResult` with boolean flags — use `CommandResult<EntityMapperResult<T>>` for saves
Use `OneOf<>` for any command return type — use `CommandResult` variants
Throw exceptions for expected domain outcomes (not found, protected, invalid state, uniqueness conflict) — use `CommandResult.Fail(...)`
Use `Guid?` with null to signal create — use `Guid.Empty` (`dto.Id == Guid.Empty`)
Use independent `SaveRequest` types — inherit from the entity DTO
Open a transaction outside EF's execution strategy in owned mode when retries are enabled.

sections.title: Command Types
content: │ Upsert (Empty?)   │  │ Bulk by Guid[]    │  │ State transition  │

sections.title: Shared Result Types
content: All non-save commands return `CommandResult` or `CommandResult<T>`. These types live in `Features/Shared/Results.cs`:
/// Structured error payload — maps directly to SendProblemAsync parameters.
public readonly record struct CommandError(string Code, string Message, int StatusCode = 400);
/// Result for commands that don't return data (delete, simple lifecycle).
public bool Succeeded => Error is null;
public static readonly CommandResult Success = new();
public static CommandResult Fail(string code, string message, int statusCode = 400)
/// Result for commands that return data (lifecycle with response DTO, action commands).
public static CommandResult<T> Ok(T value) => new() { Value = value };
// Inherits Fail() from base — returns CommandResult<T> via covariance
public new static CommandResult<T> Fail(string code, string message, int statusCode = 400)
| Single return type per command | Every command returns one type — no discriminated unions, no multi-path matching |
| No boolean flag ambiguity | `Succeeded` is derived from `Error is null` — single source of truth |
| No exceptions for expected outcomes | NotFound, protection violations, invalid state → `CommandResult.Fail(...)` |
| Self-documenting errors | `CommandError` carries Code, Message, and StatusCode — maps directly to RFC 7807 |
Error codes follow the format `{MODULE}-{NNN}` where:
- `{MODULE}` matches the feature folder name in UPPER-SNAKE-CASE (e.g., `ACTION`, `WORKFLOW`, `COMPLIANCE-PROFILE`)
- `{NNN}` is a zero-padded sequential number within the module, starting at `001`
Reserve `001`–`003` for the standard error types above. Feature-specific errors start at `004`. Each command documents its error codes in a comment block:
// ACTION-004: Action has active workflows — cannot deactivate (422)
Error codes must be unique within a module. They are used for programmatic error handling on the frontend and appear in RFC 7807 problem details responses.
### When to use exceptions vs CommandResult
| Unhandled/unexpected failure | Let exception propagate — ProblemDetailsService handles it |

**Every expected domain outcome uses `CommandResult.Fail()`** — including save commands. The only exceptions that propagate are genuinely unexpected failures (database connectivity, serialization errors, etc.).

sections.title: Mapper vs Command Boundary
content: The EntityMapper is a **pure DTO-to-Entity transformer**. The Command owns **all business logic**. This boundary is non-negotiable — see [entity-mapper.md](entity-mapper.md) for the full mapper pattern.
- Entity resolution (find by ID, business key fallback)
- DTO field to entity field mapping
- Business rules: uniqueness checks, guards, permission checks
- `SaveChangesAsync` with TOCTOU catch on unique constraints
- Post-save side effects (Redis invalidation, audit logging)
1. var mapResult = entityMapper.MapAsync(dtoList, userId, requestLanguageId, defaultLanguageId, ct, db)
2. if empty result (no NewItems, no UpdatedItems) → return Fail(404)
4. Business logic (uniqueness check → Fail(409), guards → Fail(422), cross-entity ops)

sections.title: Save Command (Primary Pattern)
content: The save command is the standard write pattern. A single command handles both insert and update, returns `CommandResult<EntityMapperResult<T>>` or a feature-specific command result, and follows the shared DbContext contract from ADR-CORE-0027.
throw new InvalidOperationException("A DbContext factory is required when no shared context is supplied.");

Commands that do not open explicit transactions may use `DbContextFactoryExtensions.ExecuteWithDbContextAsync` directly. Commands that open transactions use the explicit owned/shared branch so owned mode can run inside the EF execution strategy and shared mode can participate in the caller's current transaction.

sections.title: Delete Command
content: Delete commands accept `Guid[] keys` for bulk deletion using `ExecuteDeleteAsync`. They return `CommandResult` — protection checks return `CommandResult.Fail(...)` instead of throwing. Explicit transactions prevent partial deletes. Like save and lifecycle commands, they accept an optional `ActionsDbContext? db = null` for transaction spanning.
Guid[] keys, CancellationToken ct = default, ActionsDbContext? db = null)
// Explicit transaction — prevents partial deletes on failure
await using var tx = await db.Database.BeginTransactionAsync(ct);

sections.title: Soft Delete Command
content: Entities implementing `ISoftDeletable` (ActionEntity, ActionEvidence, ActionConsultation) are never physically deleted. Delete commands set `IsDeleted`, `DeletedDate`, and `DeletedById` instead of calling `ExecuteDeleteAsync`. The EF global query filter (`HasQueryFilter(e => !e.IsDeleted)`) excludes them from normal queries.
Guid[] keys, Guid userId, CancellationToken ct = default, ActionsDbContext? db = null)
return CommandResult.Fail("ACTION-001", "One or more actions not found.", 404);
- Loads entities (required to set `ISoftDeletable` fields) — no `ExecuteDeleteAsync`
- No explicit transaction needed — single `SaveChangesAsync` is already atomic
- To query including soft-deleted records (admin restore, audit views), use `IgnoreQueryFilters()`
See ADR-0020 for when to use soft delete vs hard delete.

sections.title: Lifecycle / Action Commands
content: These handle domain-specific operations that don't fit the save/delete pattern. They use the optional `ActionsDbContext? db = null` parameter for transaction spanning and return `CommandResult` (no data) or `CommandResult<T>` (with data).
return CommandResult.Fail("PROFILE-002", "Profile is not in draft status.", 422);
var result = await activateCommand.ExecuteAsync(id, UserId, ct);
When the caller needs the updated entity state after a lifecycle transition:

sections.title: DbContext Lifetime and Transaction Spanning
content: 1. Owned mode: the command creates a short-lived `ActionsDbContext` from `IDbContextFactory<ActionsDbContext>` and owns persistence, transaction, retry strategy, and disposal.
2. Shared mode: the caller passes `ActionsDbContext? dbContext` so multiple commands or queries can participate in one caller-owned context and transaction.

The public command constructor injects `IDbContextFactory<ActionsDbContext>`. Commands must not inject scoped `ActionsDbContext` directly. Tests may use an internal constructor with a provided context, but production registration stays factory-based.
Every DbContext-backed command accepts the optional context after the cancellation token:

Owned mode must run explicit transactions through EF Core's execution strategy. Create a separate strategy context, then create the working context inside the retry delegate so retries do not reuse a failed DbContext. Shared mode must not create or dispose the context. If the caller already opened a transaction, the command participates in it; otherwise the command may open and commit its own transaction on the supplied context when it owns the operation boundary.

sections.title: Logging
content: logger.LogDebug("Diffed workflow steps: {Added} added, {Removed} removed", addedCount, removedCount);
logger.LogWarning(ex, "Failed to invalidate cache for workflow {WorkflowId}", workflowId);
logger.LogError(ex, "Failed to notify subscribers for action {ActionId}", actionId);

sections.title: Common Mistakes
content: ### Save command returns CommandResult wrapping EntityMapperResult
// CORRECT: CommandResult<EntityMapperResult<T>> with explicit failure handling
// ... business logic, uniqueness checks return Fail on violation ...
// WRONG: Raw EntityMapperResult with ThrowError for expected outcomes
if (slugExists) ThrowError("Slug already exists.");  // ThrowError is an endpoint method, not available in commands
// create path — mapper generates new Guid.CreateVersion7()
// update path — mapper loads existing entity
### DI-registered EntityMapper vs extension method mappers
// CORRECT: Injected EntityMapper class returning EntityMapperResult<T>
// WRONG: Static extension methods — no DI, no EntityMapperResult wrapper
### SaveRequest inherits DTO vs independent SaveRequest
// WRONG: Independent request type duplicating DTO fields
public string Name { get; set; } = string.Empty;
public string Slug { get; set; } = string.Empty;
// CORRECT: ExecuteDeleteAsync — bulk server-side delete, no entity loading
// WRONG: Load entities then RemoveRange — unnecessary memory and round trips
### Delete returns CommandResult, not void or OneOf
// CORRECT: Delete returns CommandResult, protection checks return Fail
public async Task<CommandResult> ExecuteAsync(Guid[] keys, CancellationToken ct)
// WRONG: Void return with thrown exceptions for expected outcomes
public async Task ExecuteAsync(Guid[] keys, CancellationToken ct)
throw new ValidationException("Cannot delete protected entities.");
// WRONG: OneOf return for delete commands
public async Task<OneOf<Deleted, ValidationFailed>> ExecuteAsync(Guid[] keys, CancellationToken ct)

sections.title: Rules
content: 1. Use `IDbContextFactory<ActionsDbContext>` in production command constructors; never inject scoped `ActionsDbContext` directly in production services.
2. Accept `ActionsDbContext? dbContext = null` after `CancellationToken` on every DbContext-backed command execution method so callers can compose commands in a shared transaction.
3. Use a public `IDbContextFactory<ActionsDbContext>` constructor for production command DI; internal `ActionsDbContext` constructors are allowed only for focused tests.
4. In owned mode, create the working DbContext inside the EF execution-strategy delegate before beginning an explicit transaction.
5. In shared mode, use the supplied DbContext and participate in its current transaction when one exists; do not dispose caller-owned contexts.
6. Stage audit rows through `IAuditService.LogAsync(..., dbContext)` when the audit entry must commit or roll back with the aggregate mutation.
7. Use the save (upsert) pattern - determine create vs update by `dto.Id == Guid.Empty`.
8. Use DI-registered `EntityMapper` classes that return `EntityMapperResult<T>` for the mapping step in save commands.
9. Save commands return `CommandResult<EntityMapperResult<T>>` or a feature-specific `CommandResult<T>` - check `Succeeded` before accessing `Value`.
10. Delete commands return `CommandResult` - protection checks return `CommandResult.Fail(...)`.
11. Lifecycle/action commands return `CommandResult` or `CommandResult<T>` - expected errors return `Fail(...)`.
12. Check for empty mapper result on update (entity not found) - return `Fail(404)`.
13. Use `SaveRequest : EntityDTO` inheritance when the write shape matches the read DTO.
15. Wrap `SaveChangesAsync` in try/catch for expected TOCTOU unique/concurrency conflicts and return `Fail(409)`.
16. Use explicit transactions for multi-table deletes and aggregate lifecycle commands that write multiple rows.
17. Use `ExecuteDeleteAsync` for hard deletes - never load-and-delete with `RemoveRange`.
18. Use soft delete where ADR-CORE-0020 requires historical preservation.
19. Wrap non-transactional post-save side effects in individual try/catch blocks.
1. Create separate Create/Update commands - use a single Save command.
2. Use static extension method mappers - use DI-registered `EntityMapper` classes.
3. Query data for display inside commands - use the Query pattern.
4. Publish events directly from commands unless a documented feature design requires transactional outbox semantics.
5. Write audit rows directly from commands - use `IAuditService` with the shared DbContext parameter when audit is transactional.
6. Use `SaveResult` with boolean flags - use `CommandResult` variants.
7. Use `OneOf<>` for any command return type - use `CommandResult` variants.
8. Throw exceptions for expected domain outcomes.
9. Use `Guid?` with null to signal create - use `Guid.Empty`.
10. Open a transaction outside EF's execution strategy in owned mode when retries are enabled.
